topic: An employee is transferred to a different department
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official memo sat on Sarah’s desk, crisp white paper mocking her with its sterile professionalism. A new department. Marketing. It felt like a physical blow, knocking the air from her lungs. She traced the edges of the paper with a trembling finger, the fluorescent lights of the office suddenly buzzing with an unbearable intensity. Every familiar corner of the workspace—the chipped mug, the framed photo of her college friends—seemed to vibrate with an unwelcome sense of finality. When her manager, Mark, finally called her in, the silence between them stretched thin, taut as a piano wire about to snap. "It's a great opportunity, Sarah," he said, his voice unnervingly cheerful. Sarah could only manage a tight nod, her gaze fixed on the polished floor tiles as if they held the answer to an unsolvable equation.

[story 2]

"So, the shift is to Operations," Liam murmured, staring blankly at the whiteboard covered in complex flowcharts. He kept running a hand through his already dishevelled hair, the gesture jerky and repetitive. The air in the hallway felt thick, suffocating, as if the very molecules were resisting his movement. He kept replaying the conversation in his head—the casual way the announcement had been delivered, the polite, vacant smiles of his colleagues. He pushed his chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding deafeningly loud in the quiet office. He needed to leave, needed to escape the suffocating proximity of the news, needing somewhere where the boundaries of his life hadn't just been redrawn in a hostile, unfamiliar configuration.

[story 3]

"I don't understand," Maria whispered, her voice barely audible above the hum of the server room. She was clutching the strap of her messenger bag so tightly her knuckles were white. The sheer volume of the transition—the new software protocols, the entirely different client base—was overwhelming. She kept catching her reflection in the darkened monitor screen; the eyes staring back looked hollow, shadowed with an anxiety that made her stomach clench with a cold, hard knot. She began pacing the small space between the storage racks, a frantic, caged motion, unable to settle, unable to process the abrupt relocation of her entire professional existence.
